publisher.publisher-name: Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, University of Tripoli

title-group.article-title: A rare case of traumatic reticulopericarditis in camel (Camelus dromedarius)

contrib-group.aff: 1Department of Clinical Sciences, College of Veterinary Medicine, King Faisal University, Al-Ahsa, Saudi Arabia
2Institute for Research and Medical Consultations (IRMC), Imam Abdulrahman Bin Faisal University, Dammam, Saudi Arabia
3Department of Biomedical Sciences, College of Veterinary Medicine, King Faisal University, Al-Ahsa, Saudi Arabia
4Administration of Terrestrial Wildlife Conservation, National Centre for Wildlife, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

author-notes.corresp: *Corresponding Author: Turke Shawaf. Department of Clinical Sciences, College of Veterinary Medicine, King Faisal University, Al-Ahsa, Saudi Arabia. tshawaf@kfu.edu.sa

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution Non-Commercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/) which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited.

sec.title: Background:
sec.p: Traumatic reticulopericarditis (TRP) is rarely reported in camels. This report provides TRP in camels.

sec.title: Case Description:
sec.p: She-camel (3 years old) with a history of anorexia, and stinky smell-bloody diarrhea for more than 3 weeks associated with pain, ventral thoracoabdominal edema, positive for metal detection examination that raised the suspicion of TRP. The result of the plasma cardiac troponin I level was 0.253 ng/ml. Electrocardiography on admission using a standard base-apex ECG revealed an irregular rhythm of heart rate and abnormal complex morphology in QRS duration and segments (ST and TP). Elevation of the S-T segment with a slight depression in the P-R segment matched with pericarditis.

sec.title: Conclusion:
sec.p: This report suggests a rare case of TRP and demonstrated for the first time cTnI and ECG patterns of camel with TRP.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Traumatic reticuloperitonitis is a common disease of ruminants mainly dairy cattle where comprehensively occurred (Braun et al., 2020). The disease is less frequently reported in other ruminants including beef cattle, sheep, goats, and camels. Pathology of the condition includes ingestion of metallic foreign objects (MFOs) by the affected animals. The swallowed MFO settles in the reticulum and upon the functional contraction of the reticulum penetrates the wall, leading to a variety of conditions including reticulitis, reticulohepatitis, and reticuloperitonitis, according to the destination and the stage of disease (Ducharme, 1990; Braun et al., 2020). Traumatic pericarditis, an important complication of reticuloperitonitis (Braun et al., 2020), occurs when the MFO perforates the reticulum, diaphragmatic wall, epicardium, pericardium, cardiac muscle, and endocardium leading to myocarditis. Clinical signs manifested during the disease course comprise systematic symptoms such as fever tachypnea, and labored respiration; cardiovascular manifestation, including acute heart failure such as sudden death and chronic heart failure including edema of peripheral and lower limbs and multi-system failure (Radostits, et al., 2010).

An accidental traumatic reticulopericarditis (TRP) was detected during a meat inspection examination of a recumbent male camel carcass in a slaughterhouse (Hegazy et al., 1995). A recent survey of the incidence and pathology of foreign bodies in the stomach of camels (Camelus dromedarius) suggests that MFOs represent a considerable percent of the causative agent in camels reported with foreign bodies mainly pins and nails, but neither of these MFOs developed reticuloperitonitis—characterizing lesions in postmortem examinations (Raoofi et al., 2009; Sadan et al., 2020). Pica is considered a common predisposing factor that causes foreign body syndrome in camels (Kojouri et al., 2021). A recently conducted clinical trial suggested that ferroscopy, trypsin inhibitor spot test along blood examination were helpful for the diagnosis of foreign body syndrome in Kuchchhi Camels (Suthar DN et al., 2019). Clinical diagnosis of reticulopericarditis in ruminants is not always conclusive and further confirmatory diagnostic tools were claimed (Braun, 2009; Sadan et al., 2020). Once confirmed treatment options for ruminants with reticulopericarditis vary according to the prognosis between conservative, and surgical interference; otherwise, prompt humane euthanasia, especially if signs of cardiac involvement are evident (Ducharme, 1990; Braun, 2009).

sec.title: Case Details
sec.p: The case was a she-camel (3 years old) presented to the Veterinary Teaching Hospital, King Faisal University, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, weighing about 250 kg. A standard clinical examination including taking history and physical examinations was conducted. Ferroscopic examination was done to screen through the chest and abdominal cavity wall of the camel (Gerrit, 1990), for the presence of MFOs using a metal detector device (Metal detector AF 103—Whites ®, Inverness, Scotland). A standard base-apex ECG (BM7 VET; Bionet, Republic of Korea) with surface electrodes attached to the skin was used to evaluate the ECG variable’s measure of the patient camel (Al Khamis et al., 2022). ECG trace was viewed and printed on paper speed 25 mm/second. Two blood samples each 5 ml were collected from the jugular vein of the examined camel in heparinized and plain vacutainer tubes for both hematology, blood chemistry, and cTnI determination. Hematological and biochemical parameters were determined using an automated Vet scan HM5 Hematology Analyzer and Skyla-VB1 automated analyzer respectively. Cardiac troponin 1 (cTnI) was measured using a two-site sandwich-ELISA method (Tharwat, 2012) (MBS017380, Horse cTnI ELISA Kit, My Biosource. USA) range of detection is 0.25 ng/ml to 8 ng/ml and sensitivity of 0.1 ng/ml.

Standard clinical examinations revealed that the animal was alert, sternly recumbent, and emaciated. Rectal temperature was 36.5°C; pulse rate was 45 b/minute and respiratory rate of 18-cycle/minute with shallow rapid respiration. Furthermore, the texture of the camel pedestal tissue was doughy under palpation; finger press on the skin surface of this area left remarkable imprints (Fig. 1), a manifestation indicative of subcutaneous edema. Spots of tarry feces were found on the back of the trailer that was used to bring the camel to the hospital, a gross examination of which revealed the feces was stained with dark blood, indicating type II bleeding characteristic of forestomach perforating lesions.

Ferroscopic examinations (Fig. 2) revealed a loud beeping sound when a metal detector was passed over the area extending from the fifth to eighth intercostal spaces of the left side of the chest, caudal to the left elbow joint area at the level of the olecranon process. Plain chest radiography from the animal chest gave an indication of the presence of a foreign body at the second costal space as it appears in (Fig. 3), but without confirmation due to technical aspects regarding the machine.

As displayed in Figure 4, electrocardiography on admission reveals an irregular rhythm of heart rate ranging from 45–54 bpm and abnormal complex morphology in QRS duration and segments (ST and TP). In addition, complex average measures showed P–R interval (0.24 sec), QRS duration (0.12 second), S–T interval (0.44 second), Q–T interval (0.52 second), P–R segment (0.12 second), and S–T segment (0.36 second) (Fig. 4). Moreover, Peaks of p-wave (0.10 mV), Q-wave (0.30 mV), R-wave (0.50 mV), T (-)-wave (0.10 mV), and absent of S-wave peak (Fig. 4). The abnormalities of prolonged segments and intervals presented in ECG trace and the elevation of S–T segment with slight depression in P–R segment matched with pericarditis.

caption.title: Feeling edema using a finger in the chest area during the examination of the she camel is an indicator of congestive heart failure due to traumatic reticulo peritonitis. See the black arrow.

Left-side lateral Ferro-scopic examination of the chest of a she camel (Camelus dromedarius) with traumatic reticulo peritonitis presented to the Veterinary Teaching Hospital, King Faisal University KSA using Metal detector AF 103 (Whites ®, Inverness, Scotland).
Radiograph of chest showing foreign body (black arrow) at the level of second coastal space in she camel.

Standard base-apex ECG trace of the she camel (Camelus dromedarius) with traumatic reticulo peritonitis presented to the Veterinary Teaching Hospital, King Faisal University KSA. Morphology of complexes showed (-) polarity of p-wave and absence of S-peak. Additionally, variation in QT intervals and ST segments in terms of prolonged seconds. Associated short TP segments with the elevation of prolonged ST segments were noticed.

sec.p: The hematology result values were within the reference range except for RBCs count (5.36 × 109/l), hemoglobin concentration (Hb) (7.2 g/dl, and hematocrit value (HCT) 15.96 %. indicating respectively, dramatic drop-down away from reference ranges. The sharp decrease of both the total count of RBCs and hemoglobin concentration confirms severe anemia, while the low value of HCT indicates the massive loss of blood volume, however, the anemia is blood loss anemia as per type II bleeding abomasal ulcers.

The blood chemistry result showed a decrease in serum levels of albumin, total protein, glucose, blood urea nitrogen, creatinine, and sodium, while there was an increase in serum levels of alkaline phosphatase, alkaline amino transaminase, and total bilirubin. These findings suggest both liver impairment and blood loss anemia. The cTnI value was as high as 0.253 ng/ml.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The clinical examination findings suggest that the camel was suffering from the classic manifestation of TRP. Information mentioned in history that the animal was willing to eat but dropped the food off indicates that the animal suffered severe internal abdominal pain this finding is per the classic manifestation of TRP, the animal almost stopped eating despite the desire to eat to avoid the pain experienced within the swallowing. Pain was also evident within the restraining process of the animal for physical examination (Braun et al., 2020).

Moreover, tarry feces suggest type II bleeding; the dark color of the blood indicates the source of the blood from the forestomach, a manifestation common in abomasal perforation due to the ingestion of sharp MFOs (Braun, 2009). This was confirmed using the ferroscopy (Fig. 2); the device gave a loud (peep) sound over the area of the heart in the chest region close to the diaphragmatic arch, suggesting the presence of a ferromagnetic object.

Palpation of the camel pedestal tissue revealed the presence of edema (Fig. 1), a common manifestation reported in TRP indicating circulatory insufficiency due to congestive heart failure (Radostits OM. et al., 2010). Furthermore, in laboratory examinations, the hematology results disclosed that the animal suffered dropped levels of both total RBCs count, HCT, and Hb concentration. This confirms the presence of type II bleeding, due to the abomasal perforation. Moreover, circulatory involvement was observed, and the animal manifested prominent signs of chronic heart failure, this was evident in the presence of dependent organ edema (Fig. 1) supported by blood chemistry results that showed high levels of ALP, AST, and TBIL suggesting that the camel has a liver insufficiency that might be due to left-sided chronic heart failure.

In addition, the plasma cTnI level of a sample taken from the she-camel was 0.253 ng/ml three folds higher than the normal range reported in camels (Tharwat, 2012) which indicates ongoing cardiac cell damage. This finding follows previous reports of cTn-I blood levels in cattle with TRP-associated myocardial cell damage (Gunes et al., 2005). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of cTnI in a camel with TRP.

The ECG trace showed a low signal that might interfere with the prominent changes in the ECG complex and subsequently the values of measurements (Fig. 4). This could be due to the fluid accumulation in the thoracic cavity associated with the left-sided congestive heart failure due to the traumatic pericarditis; and leading to low electrical impulses received by electrodes (Fisher and Pirie, 1965). The abnormalities in complexes morphologies especially S–T segment elevation (Fig. 4) were consistent with what has been reported in cattle traumatic pericarditis cases (Fisher and Pirie, 1965) and human pericarditis cases (Radostits OM. et al., 2010). Research on camel cardiovascular disorders is very limited and to the best of the authors’ knowledge, no studies have described ECG changes of traumatic pericarditis in camel previously in the literature.

Based on these findings, the she-camel was recommended for euthanasia as per standard measures in ruminants with reticulopericarditis showing cardiac and circulatory disturbances (Ducharme, 1990; Braun, 2009). Unfortunately, postmortem examination was not affordable upon the owner’s desire. Hegazy et al. (1995) reported traumatic pericarditis in 3 years male camel at Giza abattoir in a morbid state, but based upon standard clinical examination, hematological and blood chemistry only, diagnosis of clinical cases of reticulopericarditis doubtful ruminants and camels is inconclusive (Ducharme, 1990; Braun et al., 2020; Sadan et al., 2020). Accordingly, this may be the first report of traumatic reticulo pericarditis in camel clinical case.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: A rare case of 3-year-old she camel was diagnosed with TRP associated with both cardiac and hepatic involvement, this report provides for the first time cTnI and ECG patterns of a camel with reticulopericarditis.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: The author would like to thank the authorities of the Veterinary Teaching Hospital, College of Veterinary Medicine, King Faisal University (Al-Hasa, Saudi Arabia) for their kind cooperation in conducting this valuable case report.

sec.title: Conflict of interest
sec.p: The authors declare that there is no conflict of interest.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: None.

sec.title: Data availability
sec.p: All data are provided in the manuscript.

sec.title: Authors’ contributions
sec.p: H.B, T.S, T.A, I.I, M.A, and I.E did sample collection and manuscript revision; T.S, I.E, K.A, Z.A, and M.M did supervision and manuscript preparation; H.B, T.S, T.A, and I.I did manuscript preparation and revision. H.B, T.S, T.A, and Z.A did the analysis and writing of the manuscript. All authors read and approved the final version of the manuscript.
